blurb: A bombproof native shrub — glossy trifoliate leaves that blaze orange-red in fall, tart red 'lemonade' berries, and a deep tolerance for heat, drought, and lean clay.
seasons: Yellow flower clusters in early spring before the leaves; trifoliate leaves turning brilliant orange-red in fall; fuzzy red berries summer–fall
wildlife: Early pollinators; berries and cover for birds and small mammals
gaps: No strong open-licensed fall-color photo found; the reel shows spring flowers, summer berries, and green foliage though the species is known for orange-red fall color.

care.hardiness: Tough and cold-hardy through Boulder's zone 5b–6a (rated to roughly zone 4), a widespread Western native of the plains and foothills adapted to high-altitude sun.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May last frost or in early fall on a well-drained site; it transplants well and is used widely for windbreaks and erosion control. In pots its roots are less cold-hardy, so water container plants and shelter them over winter.[1][2]
care.sun: Sun or partial shade; densest form and best fall color come in full Front Range sun.[1][2]
care.soil: Grows over a very wide range of soils, from nearly bare rock to sand to heavy alkaline clay; it only dislikes flooding and high water tables.[1]
care.water: Very drought tolerant once established; needs little to no supplemental water here, though water-stressed seedlings establish slowly.[1]
care.spacing: Give it room — it forms rounded, suckering thickets often wider than tall (to ~3–8 ft wide); space 4–6 ft apart for a screen or hedge.[1][2]
care.propagation: Seed has a hard coat and dormancy broken by cold treatment — scarify and cold-stratify, with fall/winter sowing giving best germination. It also roots readily from softwood and root cuttings (taken well before freezing) and from transplanted suckers off the spreading rhizomes.[1]
care.bloom: Small yellowish flowers open on the bare branches in early spring (April), before or with the leaves; clusters of fuzzy red berries follow in summer and persist into winter.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Low care; it suckers into thickets and sprouts vigorously from the root crown, so prune to shape or cut back to renew, and remove suckers to contain spread.[1][2]
care.harvest: Pick the tart red berries when fully colored in late summer; they persist on the shrub into winter if birds don't strip them first.[1][2]

references.name: USDA NRCS Plant Guide — Skunkbush (Rhus trilobata)
NC State Extension Plant Toolbox — Rhus trilobata

edible.summary: The tart red berries are edible and were traditionally used in foods and beverages — soaked in water to make a tangy lemonade-like drink ('sumac-ade').[1]
edible.parts: The ripe red, sparsely hairy berries (5–7 mm) are the edible part; their tartness comes from a sour surface coating that dissolves in water to flavor a drink.[1]
edible.preparation: Steep or rub the whole berries in cool water (don't boil, which can turn it bitter), then strain out the fruit and hairs; sweeten to taste for a pink lemonade-like beverage. The berries were also used in foods by Native peoples.[1]
edible.caution: This sumac is completely safe — it is NOT poison sumac (Toxicodendron vernix, a white-berried wetland tree) nor poison ivy/oak. Rhus trilobata bears upright clusters of RED berries and is harmless; only the red-fruited sumacs are used for the drink. The crushed foliage has a skunky odor (hence 'skunkbush') but is not toxic.[1][2]
